speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know it's going to fit in there, because you ask me for the details.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it's going to fit in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for the [INAUDIBLE], maybe it will. Maybe it will.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll-- we'll-- we'll go back and we'll do that story. We'll pick up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, but I mean finish the story with the time that you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, well, if you don't, we can go over. We can use more tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm going to details.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. That's fine. That's fine. We can use more tapes if you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of the interview with Yvette Lennon on February 12, 1995. All right, so you were telling me about--

words.text: so you were telling me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: So where do you want me to get--[INTERPOSING VOICES] to tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were telling me about your father teaching you [INTERPOSING VOICES] room--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that he-- so he would lock me in this little room, my father. And I-- I will come out crying. And then he will say, you crying?

speaker: interviewer
text: You think that I hate you, right? He says, but it's because I love you. He says, one day, you will remember me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what he said. And that's what I remember when this came, the accordion-- that my sister actually couldn't play the accordion, as good that after, because she knew piano so well. I told her, I'll teach you the chords. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I taught her the chords. And for the first time, I had to teach her something, you know? And she said-- she caught it in a minute. Because she was a good pianist. She knew what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she transferred-- they transfer the music. We had to play classical. And with the classical music in the accordion is not easy. But we all-- it was all arranged for the accordion so that she could play the classical.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I want to tell you about the barracks-- the music barrack. So this is where we moved in after they finished, you know, the work they had to do. And then-- so we thought it was great. We couldn't believe this was happening to us. We were very few, you know. And then transports start coming. And more girl, they would ask, if anybody can play anything, you know? [INTERPOSING VOICES] Which lager was this in, this barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was the A or the B. I think the A was the one where we were with the music, and the B was the other one, the-- the other camp. But at first, we were in-- in the camp, in one camp. And then they put us to the other camp, when you went for the music. But there were barracks there too, the same thing. It was the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know why they make lager A, lager B. Because there was no room, maybe, they had two lagers, you know? We couldn't go from one lager to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so now we started slowly an orchestra that-- [INAUDIBLE] we were in a little room at first. And that little room where at first-- you know, we first went. And-- and then they give us the real place. And we could see she was a nut. Because when she was directing, suddenly she would stop. And she would go and eat something. And then we started off playing and narrating and that. And she was-- and we were afraid of her. Because she was-- she could be very nice. And she was weird. And one day-- and now-- now they know-- they-- they-- other peoples joined us there, too, in that-- in that camp. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have more food because you were in the orchestra?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, no. Now, when we went into the orchestra in the other barrack, the-- the food was exactly the same. They did-- they give us one that tea in the morning, which was just the tea. In the-- in the middle of the day, they gave us-- excuse me. In the middle-- I have to drink some water. [DRINKS] That was in the middle of the day. And it was the-- they gave us soup. Now, we got used to the soup was-- it wasn't the greatest thing, you know. It was-- most of the time, it was water and the vegetables, you know, were few in the soup. And then they gave us, in the evening, two slices of bread with [NON-ENGLISH], what they call, [NON-ENGLISH]. The extra thing was a piece of liverwurst or salami, and you know, something in it-- that. But that wasn't enough. Because that, you can't survive on, having just a sandwich and soup in the afternoon. But at least it was something. And-- because people were-- the other people were getting the same thing, and they were starting to look like skeletons. You know, we started to look-- very thin and skeletic, but not Belsen, just in Auschwitz. Because stealing-- they-- they gave us that food there. Belsen was another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while we were-- then, every time there was a new transport, a new girl would come in. And if she played whatever she played.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, we got two Greek girls from us from Salonika, that we knew. They came after us in the camp. And they both played mandolin. So they needed mandolin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- when come some Russian girls. They were-- for political reasons, they was-- they were also playing, like, the guitars, whatever there were a few Russians. And Polish, there were also. And then they used a table. They had a big table. And that is where the copies were going to copy music. Because many times, we would get music. Like, when my brother came, he brought music. And they found the music, everything they had with them, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you copy-- they copied from there. Or they tried to give us some music.

speaker: interviewer
text: So slowly, slowly, we start playing. But it sounded terrible, the orchestra. Because she was a terrible conductor, and not everybody could play. My sister played very well the accordion, and I-- Now what do I do? What do they need me for? Now, I couldn't play like she was playing-- classical in the accordion like. Because she-- she was trained much before me. So I could play because I worked at the places that I told you, in-- in Greece-- and I learned quite a few things, like tunes, you know that. And we had to march.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to march when-- or every morning and every evening when the girls went to work, we had to go to the-- to the doors. Where they opened the doors, they should go outside the lager. And they were gonna to do their jobs. Each one had their own jobs. And-- each group, I mean. And it was the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, [NON-ENGLISH] was where they do things with clothes-- with clothes. It wasn't-- there were jobs that were better than that one that-- tanning stones. They were work inside. Each one, whatever they knew how to do, they put them in that place to work. Like, we had the orchestra-- [INAUDIBLE] it was the same with us having the orchestra. Only it was weird.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- now, as the girls were coming, there was these two girls came. And they were-- they played the mandolin, the Jewish girls and then-- from Greece-- and then there were others coming slowly. They started newcomers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I say, the conductor wasn't the greatest. And the Nazis was going to come and visit us, whenever we have-- they come to have the rest after the killing that they did. You know, that's what we say now. They're going to have some music. And they come and sit down. And they started treated-- treating us like human beings. Like, just because we play music, they thought we are humans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the others who are so-- just like us? They're treating them like garbage, you know? And-- and so suddenly, so-- but while we were, you know-- a few months pass.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were rehearsing one day, we were ready to rehearse. And suddenly, a-- a-- a-- a-- a Nazi comes in, one of the big ones-- the names, you know, there, and was-- and brings this-- this lady, a young woman. She was like-- she was shaved too and everything. She had just come into the camp-- she was like about 34 years old. And she was-- she said that she played the violin. And she-- so she sat on with a-- with a violinist. Some of the violinists we had-- see, in the orchestra, as we're getting new people, some were very well-- they knew a lot. And-- and also, they-- so it-- they could cover up the ones who didn't know so much. And before that, I forgot to tell you that we used to walk up, you know, the way up there when they went to work. And it was me who played the accordion when we marched. I was right up front. I knew so many matches by heart. And my sister would never be able to do that, because my sister could never learn things by heart. In school, all in the music, she-- she played well, she was good in school. But by heart, she couldn't retain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was good that I knew by heart. And then, they would choose-- they put me in the first here. On the first five by fives, where all- the whole orchestra was walking. And I would be up front in the middle of the two other girls, two each on each side. So I would be the accordion. Then two girls who play-- play the piccolo. Because it was three-- we had three piccolos. And later on, we got the flute.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we all we had is those three piccolos. And somebody in the back would play the drums, you know, so that we-- so that we play marching things. And the conductor was next to-- on the first row, right around the first line with the other girl. You know, all of these, we were fine. And we were walking and I was playing. It's very good that I learned so much by heart and that, they used me. Otherwise, I would have gone out to work with the other people. But they used me just for playing when we marched. We then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister during the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister just walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all walked. And-- except, well, I was the only one playing with the-- the little piccolos and the drums, you know. So that was good. But in the meantime now, I will tell you this person shows up and-- in the orchestra. And the Nazi brought her in. And we had played already for a few months. And she sits her with a fourth-- with a fourth violinist, and the conductor, the nut that we had. And she couldn't play well. And we always think, well, she can't play very well. And then she was trying to look. But they took her glasses off, you know, when she came in. And we didn't know. But the-- that-- that Nazi told her, play something for us. And she stood up and she played. We were all with our mouths open. She knew it by heart. She was a-- she was from Vienna. She had her own quartet, and her father was the-- the-- not the first violinist, the first violinist. He was the-- the main violinist, you know, in the orchestra.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- so he was there. And he was--he was very famous for-- for the Berlin orchestra, you know? And so she also had her own quartet. And I have it. I have her music from before the war. Somebody gave it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was so, so shocked when I heard her music, you know, so interested. So she said-- and she played so well, they made her the conductor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the previous conductor, you said was a Nazi or was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a nut. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A nut, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was Polish. But she was so good. But she was very good to me. She [? never ?] punished me for some little thing, or she would give me a-- [INAUDIBLE] but she-- she-- she had things to eat, you know, better than we. So she would bring me an apple. [INAUDIBLE] I was like her little one there in the orchestra that she give me things, you know, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so-- So the Germans made the new violinist--

speaker: interviewee
text: The violinist, she became the conductor and everything had changed. [INAUDIBLE] You know, they-- they had to write music. Then they brought-- other people came into the orchestra. When you have a good conductor, you play well. She told us what to do. And then the Nazis start coming in and listening to this. And Josef Mengele was there. Mengele came often. You know, you heard about him. Yeah. I didn't know that he was one of those. And-- and all the main ones, that one who was the lager-- Kramer. Kramer was the commandant. And when we were liberated in Belsen, the-- I have a tape. The British took a picture, took a-- a-- they were taping when they were liberating us. And there was Kramer coming out. They caught him. But that's another story in-- in Belsen, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the orchestra was doing pretty well. And they were coming, we were playing. And the-- then they decided that every three months, they're gonna give us a parcel of food from other people that-- the Polish people also, who were not Jewish, were in the camps for political reasons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And their parents used to send them parcels to eat-- of food. And-- but they died. They never told them that they died. They kept sending the parcels. And so they gave us one each to the orchestra. You know-- that was good, just once every three months. It wasn't that much. But it was something different to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to save the food and make it last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we tried to save it. My sister and me, we-- we-- we were sharing also. We tried to put some food for my brother, and leave somebody, you know, to share it in for three people. Because my brother was in the men's orchestra, too. So-- so we he could have-- with sometimes somebody used to go to work there. They used to say, we give you some-- some bread, or something. You give this to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did. We did share, but it wasn't that much. But every three months, it was something, a little change. So in the meantime, time was passing. And this last person were coming. Every-- they started to come faster, because we're coming towards the end of the war. I'm not touching here, am I? The end of the war. And so suddenly, I see this girl coming in. And this girl, she came into us. And she says, what is lager? What is it? We didn't tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: We already knew they were gas chambers. The gas chambers were very close to the orchestra. You see, where we were, they-- there's a-- [INAUDIBLE] our camp, I told you, there's two separations. There was the camping between the-- us. There was the two camps. But say, when we start coming out, supposedly, we want to play right at the end of the-- of the-- of that crossing. That's where we stood, playing until they went to work. Then they opened the gates. Right there were the gates in between the two camps, you know that. These gates was the train stations. That's why the trains were coming, you know. And-- and the trains would stop there. But they usually would do it at night, so we don't see what's going on. But after that, say, if you walk a block from here or two blocks at the most, the gas chambers were there. And we could see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these gas chambers, they-- we-- they never did it at night when the people-- they always did it at night so that the people-- we won't see it. We were in bed, when it's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they will have the chambers and have that. We never witnessed it. But we witnessed it in the very last days. That's another story. But the thing is that, that day, a transport came. And that was my father and mother. That was the last one was coming from Greece. But we didn't know who they were. But this girl who walked in into the-- she was 16 years old. She walked into the camp. And she said, she-- she-- my father-- my brother was friends with her father. Her father was 50 years old, but he was a doctor in Greece, a very good doctor. And he played the flute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother used to play with him at his house. So they were in the same transport. They let him in, even he was almost 50. Because he said he was a doctor and a musician.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept him. And they put him into the orchestra. But also, they make him for things that people were sick, they make him use it. They used him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so my brother, he-- he told me-- that girl-- her name was Lena-- told me that, I came with your father and your mother. I said, you did? We already knew about the gas chambers then. I said, where are they? Where did they go? We wanted to know if they went on the bus or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, well, it's OK. My father and-- and your brother, they walked. That means they came into the camp, the two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say, your mother and my mother, they went on the bus, I'll say. And she says, where are they going? And my sister and me looked at each other. Because we didn't want to tell her they're going to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we went back, oh, we were so upset. We [INAUDIBLE] so [INAUDIBLE] upset-- [INAUDIBLE] to see, we knew then, that was the day that-- that-- they died. We knew it. And they did it in the night, I guess. That's what happened. Because by the time they brought her in, it was already day time, you know, until they go through all this-- what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it means that we knew already. That was the last transport from Greece. And-- and then, my brother went into the orchestra, because he-- he said he was a musician.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him. But the orchestra didn't have-- their orchestra didn't have the same privileges we had. They made them work half of the day. And they was chosen for-- for instance, every-- every three or four months in-- it-- you know, an order would come from Germany that the people, the girls who had been working in the camp, now they're too sick-- look sick or have things in her-- their body, stains-- you know, things like that. They said that they have to go straight to the gas chambers. And so-- so what they did, is they-- they used to take them in the camp in the morning, and late in the evening, and they used it all the time that we were all in bed. And they had them naked. And they would see what they have, if they have anything wrong with their bodies, or they look-- they are too thin. Then they choose which ones will go to the gas chamber. They already knew, the girls, that there is gas chamber. Was-- that was the toughest thing. And one-- the two-- two girls went to the gas chamber were my first cousins, these pictures I showed you. They were my first cousins. Because they chose them. They put them-- they put them in a special block. I forget the name they call the block. And that is the block that-- they-- they had them there for three days. I don't know whether they gave them to eat, they didn't. And then they go to the gas chambers at night. So-- but you don't know who's there, who went. Now, I-- I have a friend-- I showed you the picture from Israel, she's now in Israel, but she was with me. She went to the concentration camp with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She came also after me. And which-- you smell burn? What is it from? I don't know. Do you want to stop taping for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not from--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I talk now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my friend Olga, we were very close friends in Greece. And she spoke English beautifully too, because she went to the American school-- school before me. But then I joined her. And we-- we came very close. And when we went into the camp, it's like she was-- like we were sisters. She had her other older sister, who was 24. Her name was Flora. And she was in the block, working with my-- my cousins, too, in the same block. I had two cousins-- Nita and Frida. And-- and they had a brother younger than them, Chaim. Chaim, I heard, he was castrated. He was 19 years old, or 18. So Frida and Nita was with us. And-- but they too, they didn't know music or they didn't know the things. They started to be, you know, looking sick. And-- and what happened is that that day-- I want to say that all of those that I mentioned, they were chosen for block 25. That was the name. We knew there was a block 25. And they put them-- they put them to the gas chambers that night. But I didn't know, we didn't know because they were in the next camp-- lager-- and we didn't know what's happening there. But Olga knew, because she worked for this place, Kanada-- she worked at the railroad station. So she met her people like men knew what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was Kanada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kanada was called that the best work in the-- in the camp. It means that when you-- remember when we came in with the transports and we had all the food outside and all our-- not just that, the jewelry? They-- they put young men to-- to fill out the sacks and everything to bring for the Germans. And-- and sometimes, like, my-- my-- my friend Olga was there. And she-- they put girls to work. And she used to hide things sometimes. They used to hide it inside the blouses and things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes, she used to bring the things, things for me, you know, that she caught to eat and that. So-- and they call it Kanada, because it's the richest place to work for. I said, why do they call it Kanada? It's just because it was like the rich country, they-- where they were working, you know? Just, that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Olga was working from-- she must have found out from some men, because men work there too. And she tells me-- she was crying. She says, Yvette, you don't know what happened. Your sister-- you say, my sister Flora, and your two cousins, they're going to the gas chambers today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? She says, [? I said ?] yes. She was crying, she was crying, and I was crying. And she says to me, now I'm all alone, I say, you-- I say, I'm your sister now. I say, we're sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, that's why we're so close up til now. Even if she's in Israel, we went through hell together. And-- and they went. We didn't know. We didn't know until-- because we found out from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the worst part was that when we went back to Greece-- see, I'll go backward because it relates with the story. When we went back to Greece, and one of the girls who made it told us, you know your cousin Frida? She says, she didn't have to go to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's your cousin Nita who had to go. But they-- she decided to go and die with her sister. And she gave me the place. I was the one to go to the gas chamber. See, she wanted to die with her sister. She let that girl come out and stay alive. She says, that's why I'm here. So you see, we learned some one to the other what happened. So-- so-- and then-- then as time was passing already and we were giving concerts. And and one day-- oh, then they needed a double bass very-- very much. They said the double bass is important into an orchestra. We had a fantastic cellist. She was from Berlin and she-- now she's alive. She just came here to give a concert, and I went to-- to see her, you know, with the English chamber orchestra. She says, funny how the world is small.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I saw they're playing, I had-- because my son plays with the opera, played here at [INAUDIBLE], he's a violist. So when I-- I read in the paper that-- in the book that the English chamber orchestra is coming here. And I say, is Anita coming? And I wrote her a letter. And I say, are you coming? She says, she wrote me, yes, and you better be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met after all these years, and we were hugging each other and everything, you know. And we talked about the camp a bit. She-- you know, twice I saw her. When she came at Carnegie Hall also, we met her. That's another story that she told my-- my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: See, I told you we had a famous pianist. Murray Perahia, he's a Sephardic Jew-- he's very famous. My sister taught him. I-- I did not show you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- my sister was his teacher. And he became one of the most famous pianists in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, your sister was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when she came here--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --started to be a teacher. And suddenly, we-- and then he became the-- he became the conductor of the English chamber orchestra, and a pianist in England. While I was in England, I didn't know that Anita was living just around the corner from me, you know. And Anita was part of it. So, then this Murray Perahia got married with a-- an Israeli girl. And the wedding was in England. Anita was there. And she says, then Murray says, tell me, Anita. You said that you were playing in an orchestra in the concentration camp. Well, my piano teacher was also playing in an orchestra with her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is her name Lily? She says, yes. She says, give me her phone number.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and that's when we got a phone call. And we all met at Carnegie Hall. That was 20 years ago. And now I met again here with her, you know. So, she was a cellist. She was so good. She was like a bit older than me. She was 17. And she-- by the way, we all had typhus while we were there. My Nita was the first one to have typhus. She made it, and she came home. We were all dying because of the water. It was bad. We-- we were not supposed to drink the water. It was green. You're supposed to boil it. It was very bad water. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was no way to boil it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And the people would say, we wouldn't drink it much. You know, others would drink it. And we drank the tea that they gave us, cause already boiled. We wouldn't drink the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one by one, they were going to-- some died already. We had-- the opera singers, we had about five singers in the orchestra and they were all fantastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was from the Berlin Philharmonic. She was Jewish. Doris was her name. She had typhus and she died while we were in the orchestra. Then-- then I was one that had the typhus. And I-- they-- they put me to the hospital, but they didn't give me much of anything. Just one pill. And I was delirious, and I could not think carefully. So that week-- and I didn't eat anything. The second week, they started to give me a little food. And I try-- I said to myself, I have to force myself to eat or I'm gonna die. By the end of the second week, I still was very sick. So it occurred to me that, if an order comes from Germany that they're going to kill-- they pick up 500 girls for the gas chambers, I'll be one of them. The hospital comes first. So just get rid of the sick ones. Immediately, I said to myself. I'm going to get out of here. I'm not going to stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the nurse came and she says-- I say, I'm fine. I say, I'm going to leave. I've got to leave now. She says, you're not. You're not well, yet. I said, I'm really feel good. So she say, she put the thermometer-- and I let it pass, you know, further down here in the arm. She says, all right. You can go. Go to the other bed. Tomorrow, you leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went, I didn't even know. I thought I saw the start, everything black. I couldn't walk from here to there. But I say I'm leaving. The next morning, I went. In the meantime, my sister was crying. She thought I was dead because she come near the hospital. And every time, she saw somebody dead coming out, she saw somebody with black hair. By the way, they let us have the hair in the orchestra, to a certain point, you know-- up to short, because we were in the orchestra. They didn't cut it anymore. And she-- she said, my sister is dead. My sister is dead. She was crying all the time they told me, the girls. And that day, I showed up-- they were playing. I was alone there and sitting. They must have gone out to play. When she came, she was so excited. She says to me, you're alive. I say, don't tell anybody. I say, I don't really feel good yet. She says, don't worry, I'll take care of you. She says-- and like this, slowly, I got out of it. But I made it with the typhus. That was the one thing I made, the first thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the nurse a German nurse... or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the nurse were-- were prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of them was a Greek nurse. She was telling me, you go here. I'll-- I'll-- I'll take care of you. I'll take care of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, no. I say, if an order comes for the gas chambers, you think they'll let you-- me take care of you? I say, I've got to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left. I used my mind, I say, to survive. You know, I say it's better than in the back, that going. See, I was recovering. I was very weak and that. But there-- one of the two first weeks was the worst. So nobody got it from me, because I was-- I was almost recovered. But I was so weak I couldn't see well and everything, you know. It took me another week to really-- and then I had to-- I was forced to play, though. Because once you go there, you play.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, they needed-- the orchestra was getting bigger and bigger. They needed a double bass. There was a double bass in the corner in the room, because they brought instruments from people who left their instruments.

speaker: interviewee
text: And nobody could play the double bass. So now I'm going backwards in Greece-- sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's gone already.
